attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of worsening shortness of breath, headache, and blurred vision. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C. She has scattered petechiae on her trunk and limbs. Chest auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Neurological examination is unremarkable except for bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 85 g/L (115-165)

  • White cell count: 320 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Platelet count: 45 x 10^9/L (150-400)

  • Peripheral blood film: Numerous blast cells present
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis in this case is leucostasis. This condition is a medical emergency characterised by symptoms of hyperviscosity due to extremely high white blood cell counts, typically >100 x 10^9/L. In this case, the patient's white cell count is markedly elevated at 320 x 10^9/L, and the peripheral blood film shows numerous blast cells, suggesting acute leukaemia.

The patient's presenting symptoms are classic for leucostasis: respiratory distress (shortness of breath, hypoxia, bilateral crackles), neurological symptoms (headache), and visual disturbances (blurred vision, papilloedema). These symptoms occur due to sludging of the hyperviscous blood in the microvasculature of the lungs, brain, and eyes.

Acute bacterial meningitis could present with headache and fever, but would not explain the respiratory symptoms, visual changes, or the markedly elevated white cell count with blast cells.

Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis could cause headache and papilloedema but would not account for the respiratory symptoms or the haematological abnormalities.

Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL) is characterised by mature-appearing lymphocytes rather than blast cells. The acute onset of symptoms and the presence of blast cells make acute leukaemia with leucostasis more likely than CLL.

Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura can present with neurological symptoms and thrombocytopenia but would not cause such a high white cell count or the presence of blast cells.

The presence of blast cells on the peripheral blood film, along with anaemia and thrombocytopenia, strongly suggests acute leukaemia as the underlying condition. The extremely high white cell count and the constellation of symptoms affecting multiple organ systems make leucostasis the most likely diagnosis. Urgent treatment with cytoreduction (e.g., leukapheresis or hydroxyurea) is necessary to prevent further complications such as stroke or respiratory failure.
learningPoints: Leucostasis is a medical emergency characterised by symptoms of hyperviscosity due to extremely high white blood cell counts, typically >100 x 10^9/L.
Common presenting features of leucostasis include respiratory distress, neurological symptoms, and visual disturbances.
Peripheral blood film showing blast cells is crucial for diagnosing leucostasis in the context of acute leukaemia.
references: Röllig C, Ehninger G. How I treat hyperleukocytosis in acute myeloid leukemia. Blood. 2015;125(21):3246-3252. doi:10.1182/blood-2014-10-551507
